key_insight: In a real-world cancer registry task, larger LLMs generally matched human-abstraction labels better than smaller models, especially for simpler registry fields.
clinical_relevance: LLMs could speed up cancer registry abstraction and reduce manual workload, but date-related and other high-precision fields still need careful human oversight.
method: Evaluated 5 foundational LLMs with zero-shot prompting
Abstracted 8 registry variables from clinical reports
Tested across 5,939 patients and 7 cancer types
evidence_signal: Using hospital cancer registry data as ground truth, the best models reached about 0.8 F1 for low-cardinality variables and about 0.55 exact accuracy for diagnosis and treatment dates, improving to about 0.85 within ±30 days.
watch_item: Performance varied by variable and cancer type, so deploying these models as standalone abstraction tools could introduce errors without validation and review workflows.
paper_title: Large language models for cancer registry abstraction: a real-world evaluation across models, variables, and cancer types
